title: Logging and diagnostics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Logging and diagnostics; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; typing, unittest, logging, pdb, and development-tools documentation; atomic topic: Logging and diagnostics
sources.claim: Python logging emits severity-appropriate structured operational events through configured handlers, keeping diagnostics separate from user output and excluding secrets or excessive personal data.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python logging emits severity-appropriate structured operational events through configured handlers, keeping diagnostics separate from user output and excluding secrets or excessive personal data.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A service logs a safe request id, operation, and failure category with logger.exception while the user receives a bounded message. Your goal is to add useful operational logging and distinguish it from user-facing output. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Project metadata and command entry; Exceptions, files and resources; Classes and object protocols; Exception handling and propagation; Packages and public APIs. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python logging emits severity-appropriate structured operational events through configured handlers, keeping diagnostics separate from user output and excluding secrets or excessive personal data. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: String interpolation can happen before filtering, handlers can duplicate messages, exception logging may expose inputs, library code should not own global configuration, and logs are not control flow. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Debug level permits passwords, tokens, or raw learner data because production normally filters those records.

includes: Emit structured severity-appropriate diagnostics without leaking secrets or replacing exception handling.
Add useful operational logging and distinguish it from user-facing output.
excludes: String interpolation can happen before filtering, handlers can duplicate messages, exception logging may expose inputs, library code should not own global configuration, and logs are not control flow.

outcomes: Add useful operational logging and distinguish it from user-facing output.

examples.title: Logging and diagnostics: direct language case
examples.situation: A service logs a safe request id, operation, and failure category with logger.exception while the user receives a bounded message.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A service logs a safe request id, operation, and failure category with logger.exception while the user receives a bounded message.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Never place secrets in log arguments, use structured safe context and appropriate severity, and configure handlers at the application boundary.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Never place secrets in log arguments, use structured safe context and appropriate severity, and configure handlers at the application boundary.

examples.title: Logging and diagnostics: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: An API token and full payload are excluded or redacted even during debug logging, and a test asserts the secret never enters captured logs.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: An API token and full payload are excluded or redacted even during debug logging, and a test asserts the secret never enters captured logs.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: String interpolation can happen before filtering, handlers can duplicate messages, exception logging may expose inputs, library code should not own global configuration, and logs are not control flow.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Debug level permits passwords, tokens, or raw learner data because production normally filters those records.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Add useful operational logging and distinguish it from user-facing output.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Debug level permits passwords, tokens, or raw learner data because production normally filters those records.
misconceptions.correction: Never place secrets in log arguments, use structured safe context and appropriate severity, and configure handlers at the application boundary.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Debug level permits passwords, tokens, or raw learner data because production normally filters those records.

analogy.example: an inspected delivery package whose interface notes, repeatable checks, safe incident log, and clean installation prove bounded quality claims can introduce Logging and diagnostics, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: typing, tests, logging, debugging, and project completion
limitations: annotations do not enforce runtime types, mocks can mislead, logs can leak, and local editable installs can hide packaging defects

recap.summary: You should now be able to add useful operational logging and distinguish it from user-facing output. Keep this boundary in view: String interpolation can happen before filtering, handlers can duplicate messages, exception logging may expose inputs, library code should not own global configuration, and logs are not control flow.
